1: Eikhah yaib Adonai be-appo -- 'How the Lord has covered Daughter Zion with his anger.' Ch 2 opens with the same eikhah as ch 1, but the perspective shifts dramatically: where ch 1 described the suffering primarily from Jerusalem's perspective, ch 2 focuses on YHWH as the active agent of destruction. Yaib (H5743, to cover with clouds/darken) -- YHWH's anger is like a storm cloud covering the city. Hishlik mi-shamayim eretz tiferet Yisrael -- 'he cast down from heaven to earth the splendor of Israel.' The tiferet (glory/beauty/splendor) of Israel -- likely the Temple and its associated glory -- is hurled downward. The fall is literally from heaven to earth, a reversal of the Temple's sacred orientation. Ve-lo zakhar hadom raglav be-yom appo -- 'and did not remember his footstool on the day of his anger.' The footstool (hadom raglav) of YHWH is the Temple (Ps 99:5; 132:7; 1 Chr 28:2) -- the place of his earthly throne-presence, now forgotten in wrath.
2: Billa YHWH ve-lo chamal -- 'The Lord swallowed up without mercy.' Billa (H1104, to swallow up/engulf) -- the verb of total destruction; nothing remains after swallowing. Lo chamal (he did not spare/had no mercy) -- the absence of the compassion (rachamim) that normally characterizes YHWH. Et kol-neot Yaaqov -- 'all the dwelling places of Jacob.' Neot (H4999, pastures/dwelling places) -- the inhabited land of the covenant people. Haras be-evrato mivtzarei bat-Yehudah -- 'he broke down in his wrath the strongholds of Daughter Judah.' Haras (H2040, to tear down/demolish) -- the military destruction of fortifications. The word appears repeatedly in this chapter. Higa la-aretz chilelah mamlakhtah ve-sareha -- 'he has brought down to the ground in dishonor the kingdom and its princes.' The royal institutions have been brought low.
3: Gada be-charon af kol-qeren Yisrael -- 'He cut off in fierce anger all the strength of Israel.' Gada (H1438, to cut off/fell) -- the verb for cutting down a tree; Israel's military strength is felled like timber. Qeren (H7161, horn) -- the symbol of power and strength; all of Israel's strength is cut at once. Heishiv achor yemino mifnei oyev -- 'he withdrew his right hand from before the enemy.' The yamin (right hand) is YHWH's weapon hand, his hand of saving power. Its withdrawal is the decisive moment: YHWH stops fighting for Israel and begins fighting against them. Vayivar be-Yaaqov ke-esh lehevah -- 'and he burned through Jacob like a flaming fire.' The divine fire imagery from ch 1:13 recurs -- but now the source (YHWH himself burning like a flame) is fully explicit.
4: Darakh qashto ke-oyev -- 'He bent his bow like an enemy.' The theological crisis of ch 2 reaches its apex: YHWH acts as oyev (enemy) against his own people. Nitzav yemino ke-tzar -- 'he set his right hand like a foe.' The same right hand that was Israel's salvation (Exod 15:6; Ps 118:15-16) is now set as an adversary. Va-yaharog kol machmadei ayin -- 'and he killed all who were precious to the eye.' Machmadim (precious things/beloved ones) -- the recurring word now for people rather than objects: the beloved ones whom YHWH himself slew. Be-ohel bat-Tziyyon shafakh ke-esh chamato -- 'in the tent of Daughter Zion he poured out his wrath like fire.' YHWH is not merely permitting the enemy's destruction but actively pouring out fury within the sacred precincts.
5: Hayah YHWH ke-oyev -- 'The LORD has become like an enemy.' The starkest theological statement in the book. Ke-oyev (like an enemy) -- the particle ke (like) is important: YHWH is compared to an enemy in his actions, not identified as one essentially. But the functional equivalence is real and devastating. Billa Yisrael -- 'he swallowed up Israel.' The billa of v.2 returns. Billa kol-armenotehah -- 'he swallowed up all her palaces.' Armenotot (H759, palaces/citadels) -- the structures of royalty and governance consumed. Ve-yarbeh be-bat-Yehudah taaniyah va-aniyah -- 'and he has multiplied mourning and lamentation in Daughter Judah.' Taaniyah (H8589, lamentation) and aniyah (H592, sighing/groaning) -- a pair of mourning terms.
6: Vayachmos ke-gan sukko -- 'He has stripped his booth like a garden.' Sukko (his booth/shelter) refers to the Temple as YHWH's dwelling place -- the same vocabulary as Ps 27:5 and 76:3. The garden stripping suggests violent clearance. Shichet moado -- 'he has destroyed his meeting place.' Moed again -- the appointed-feast assembly, but now it is YHWH who destroys his own meeting place. Hishkach YHWH be-Tziyyon moed ve-Shabbat -- 'YHWH has caused Zion to forget the appointed feast and the Sabbath.' Hishkach (to cause to forget) -- YHWH himself erases the liturgical calendar, the rhythm of covenant worship. The Sabbath and festival cycle that structured Israel's life is suspended. Vayinatz be-zaam appo melekh ve-kohen -- 'and in his angry indignation he has spurned king and priest.' Both the royal and priestly institutions are objects of divine contempt.
7: Zanach YHWH mizbecho -- 'The Lord has rejected his altar.' Zanach (H2186, to reject/cast off) -- a strong term for absolute repudiation; the same verb used in Ps 77:8 for the fear that YHWH has forever cast off. The altar -- the central locus of Israel's sacrificial worship and atonement -- is rejected by the one to whom it was directed. Nir miqdash -- 'he has disowned his sanctuary.' Nir (H5010, to abhor/disown) -- the complete reversal of the Temple's consecration. Higger be-yad oyev chomat armenotehah -- 'he has delivered into the hand of the enemy the walls of her palaces.' What the covenant was meant to protect has been handed over to the very enemy it was meant to exclude. Qol natenu be-veit-YHWH ke-yom moed -- 'they raised a shout in the house of the LORD as on the day of appointed feast.' The enemy's battle cry in the Temple sounds like a feast-day shout -- a terrible inversion of sacred sound.
8: Chashav YHWH le-hashchit chomat bat-Tziyyon -- 'The LORD determined to lay in ruins the wall of Daughter Zion.' Chashav (H2803, to plan/design/reckon) -- the same word used for skilled craftsmanship (Exod 31:4) and for theological recounting; here it is YHWH's deliberate architectural plan -- but for demolition. Natah qav lo-heishiv yado mi-balla -- 'he stretched out the measuring line; he did not restrain his hand from swallowing.' The qav (measuring line) is the builder's tool -- but here stretched out by a divine demolisher measuring what he will destroy (cf. Amos 7:7-9, the plumb line). Va-yaavel chel ve-chomah -- 'and he made rampart and wall mourn.' Even the fortification structures participate in the lament.
9: Tabeu va-aretz sharehah -- 'Her gates have sunk into the ground.' The gates -- the place of justice, commerce, and assembly -- are not merely broken but have sunk below the surface, the inverse of the city built on a hill. Ibad u-shiver berichehah -- 'he has destroyed and broken her bars.' The bars (berichin) that locked the gates for defense are shattered. Malkah ve-sareha baggoyim ein Torah -- 'her king and princes are among the nations; the Torah is no more.' The two institutions of covenant governance -- king and Torah -- are simultaneously lost: the king in exile, the Torah without teacher or Temple context to enact it. Gam-neviehah lo matzeu chazon me-YHWH -- 'also her prophets find no vision from YHWH.' The prophetic word has ceased -- the silence of YHWH is itself a form of judgment.
10: Yeshvu la-aretz yidmemu ziqnei bat-Tziyyon -- 'The elders of Daughter Zion sit on the ground in silence.' Yidmemu (H1826, to be silent/still/dumb) -- the silence that is the absence of wisdom's speech. The elders, whose role was counsel and judgment at the gate, sit mute in the dust. Alu afar al-rosham -- 'they have thrown dust on their heads.' The gesture of mourning: dust or ashes on the head (Josh 7:6; Job 2:12; Neh 9:1). Chatru saqim betulot Yerushalayim -- 'the virgins of Jerusalem have bowed their heads to the ground.' Chatru (bowed down/girded in sackcloth) -- the double gesture of grief: posture and garment. The book's most vulnerable figures -- the young women who represent future and fertility -- are prostrated in mourning.
11: Kalu ve-demaot einai -- 'My eyes are spent with weeping.' Kalu (H3615, to be spent/exhausted/completed) -- the eyes have reached their limit of tears. The observer's own weeping begins now, shifting from third-person description to first-person participation in the grief. Chamarmaru meai -- 'my stomach churns.' The same chamarmaru as 1:20 -- the physical manifestation of grief in the gut. Nishpakh la-aretz kevodi -- 'my bile is poured on the ground.' Kaved (liver/bile, H3516) -- the ancients considered the liver the seat of emotion; its pouring out = total emotional depletion. Al-shever bat-ammi -- 'because of the destruction of Daughter my-people.' Shever (H7667, breaking/destruction) -- a decisive breaking. The phrase bat-ammi (Daughter of my people) expresses intimate identification: not merely 'the people' but 'my people's daughter.'
12: Le-imotam yomeru ayeh dagan va-yayin -- 'They cry to their mothers: Where is bread and wine?' The particular horror of famine during siege: children crying for basic food. Dagan (grain/bread) and yayin (wine) -- the staples of covenant blessing (Deut 7:13; 11:14). Their absence represents the inversion of covenant promise. Be-hithatpam ke-chalal bi-rechovot ha-ir -- 'as they faint like the wounded in the streets of the city.' Chalal (H2491, wounded/slain) -- the children's fainting from hunger is compared to warriors fallen in battle. The domestic space of the city has become a battlefield. Be-hishta-peak nafesham el-cheq imotam -- 'as their soul is poured out into their mothers' bosoms.' The dying children collapse onto their mothers -- an image of total helplessness combined with the final refuge of maternal embrace.
13: Mah-aidekh mah-adammeh lakh ha-betulat bat-Tziyyon -- 'What can I bear witness against you? What can I compare to you, O virgin Daughter Zion?' The observer's question is not rhetorical mockery but genuine perplexity: there is no adequate comparison for this suffering. Ki-gadol ka-yam shivrekh -- 'for great as the sea is your ruin.' Shever (breaking/destruction, H7667) -- the same word as v.11. The comparison to the sea (yam) -- limitless, unmeasurable -- indicates that the catastrophe exceeds normal frameworks of comparison. Mi-yirpa lakh -- 'who can heal you?' Rafa (H7495, to heal) -- healing is the word of covenant restoration; but who can heal a wound of ocean dimensions?
14: Neviayikh chazu lakh shav va-tafel -- 'Your prophets have seen for you false and deceptive visions.' Shav (H7723, emptiness/falsehood/vanity) -- the same word used in the third commandment ('do not take the name of YHWH in vain' / la-shav); prophets who spoke in YHWH's name without divine authority. Tafel (H8602, insipid/daubed/whitewashed) -- false prophecy as whitewashing over cracks, giving a false impression of structural soundness. Ve-lo gilu al-avonekh le-hashiv shevutekh -- 'they did not expose your iniquity to restore your fortune.' True prophecy addresses sin in order to enable restoration; false prophecy avoids it and leaves the people without warning or opportunity for repentance. Vayechezu lakh massaot shav u-maduchim -- 'they saw false oracles and causes of banishment for you.' The false prophets' visions contributed to the exile they were supposed to prevent.
15: Sapequ alayikh kappayim kol-ovrei derekh -- 'All who pass by clap their hands at you.' Where 1:12 appealed to passers-by for sympathy, here the passers-by respond with mockery -- clapping hands (sapequ kappayim) as a gesture of contempt and derision. Sharqu vayanu rosham al bat-Yerushalayim -- 'they hiss and wag their heads at Daughter Jerusalem.' Sharqu (to hiss) -- a sound of scorn and shock. Hazot ha-ir asher yomeru kelilat yofi -- 'Is this the city that was called the perfection of beauty?' The quotation mocks the theological claim of Jerusalem's unique beauty (Ps 48:2 -- 'beautiful in elevation, the joy of all the earth'). What was acclaimed as perfect has been reduced to ruin. Mesos le-khol-ha-aretz -- 'the joy of all the earth' -- now an object of scorn rather than joy.
16: Patztu alayikh pihhem kol-oyvayikh -- 'All your enemies open their mouths against you.' Patztu pihhem (opened their mouth wide) -- the gaping mouth of contempt and triumph. Sharqu va-yachrequ shen -- 'they hiss and gnash their teeth.' Charaqu shen (gnashing teeth) -- the aggressive physical expression of hostility and derision (Ps 35:16; 37:12). Amru bilanu -- 'they say: We have swallowed her up.' The same bila verb used of YHWH's action in vv. 2, 5 -- now the enemies claim the same act as their own. Akh zeh ha-yom shequivvinu -- 'surely this is the day we looked for.' The enemies' long-anticipated opportunity has arrived. Matzanu rainu -- 'we found it; we saw it.' The gleeful confirmation of Jerusalem's destruction.
17: Asah YHWH asher zamam -- 'The LORD has done what he purposed.' Zamam (H2161, to plan/devise/purpose) -- YHWH's deliberate intention; nothing has happened accidentally. Bitza imrato asher tzivvah mi-mei qedem -- 'he has carried out his word that he commanded long ago.' The destruction is the execution of the covenant curses announced in Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 -- commands given at Sinai, centuries before their fulfillment. Haras ve-lo chamal -- 'he tore down without mercy.' The same lo chamal of v.2. Vayesamach alayikh oyev -- 'and he caused the enemy to rejoice over you.' YHWH is the cause even of the enemy's rejoicing -- a further dimension of the theological crisis. Herim qeren tzarayikh -- 'he exalted the horn of your adversaries.' The horn (power) that was supposed to belong to Israel (Ps 89:17) has been transferred to the enemy.
18: Tzaaq libam el-YHWH -- 'Their heart cried to the LORD.' Suddenly a call to prayer emerges. Chomat bat-Tziyyon -- 'O wall of Daughter Zion.' The wall itself is addressed as a mourner and intercessor. Horidi ka-nachal dimah yomam va-lailah -- 'let tears stream down like a river day and night.' Ka-nachal (like a wadi/stream) -- the image of tears as a never-stopping watercourse. Day and night (yomam va-lailah) -- total, unceasing lament. Al-titni fugat lakh -- 'give yourself no rest.' Fugat (H6314, respite/rest) -- the call to relentless prayer. Al-tidom bat-einekh -- 'let not your eye's daughter be still.' The 'daughter of your eye' -- the pupil -- should not stop weeping. The intensity of the call matches the severity of the crisis.
19: Qumi roni va-layl -- 'Arise, cry out in the night.' The call to night-prayer -- the watches of the night when grief is most acute and the cry most desperate. Le-rosh ashmurot -- 'at the beginning of the night watches.' The night was divided into three or four watches; the first watch (beginning of the night) is when prayer should begin, not wait. Shiphki ka-mayim libbekh nochach penei YHWH -- 'pour out your heart like water before the presence of the LORD.' Shaphak lev (to pour out the heart) -- total self-disclosure in prayer, holding nothing back. Nochach penei YHWH -- 'before the face of YHWH' -- the prayer is directed at the divine presence. Sei elav kappayikh -- 'lift your hands to him.' The gesture of prayer -- lifted palms. Al-nefesh olalelayikh -- 'for the souls of your young children who are fainting with hunger at every street corner.'
20: Reh YHWH ve-habbitah le-mi olalta koh -- 'Look, O LORD, and see! With whom have you dealt thus?' The direct appeal to divine witness. Habbitah (H5027, to look/gaze with attention) -- to look carefully, not casually. The question le-mi olalta koh (to whom have you done thus) is not a challenge to divine justice but a cry of bewilderment: this is your covenant people -- this is what has happened. Im-tokhal nashim piryam -- 'Should women eat the fruit of their womb?' The horror of cannibalism during the siege is introduced (cf. Deut 28:53-57; Jer 19:9; Ezek 5:10) -- the most extreme symbol of total social collapse. Im-yehareg be-miqdash YHWH kohen ve-navi -- 'should priest and prophet be killed in the sanctuary of the LORD?' The Temple, the last refuge, has become a killing floor.
21: Shakhvu la-aretz chutztot naar ve-zaqen -- 'In the dust of the streets lie young and old.' The dead lie unburied -- the extremity of desolation. Betulotai u-vachuray naflu ve-charev -- 'my young women and young men have fallen by the sword.' The young generation -- the future -- is slain. Haragtah be-yom appekha tavachta lo chamaltha -- 'you killed them in the day of your anger; you slaughtered without mercy.' The theological claim remains: YHWH is the agent. Tavachta (H2873, to slaughter/butcher) -- the vocabulary of the slaughterhouse applied to the divine action. Lo chamaltha -- the lo chamal of vv. 2, 17 -- three times in this chapter the absence of divine mercy is named. The covenant compassion (rachamim) has been suspended.
22: Tiqra ke-yom moed megorai mi-saviv -- 'You summoned as on a day of appointed feast my terrors from every side.' The final verse uses the moed motif one last time -- but reversed. YHWH calls together ('summons' like a festival assembly) Jerusalem's terrors (megorai, H4032, terrors/fears) from every direction. The feast-day convocation is now a gathering of horrors. Ve-lo hayah be-yom af-YHWH palith ve-sarid -- 'and on the day of the LORD's anger no one escaped or survived.' The totality of destruction -- palith (escapee) and sarid (survivor/remnant) -- both categories are emptied. Asher tiladti ve-ribbiti -- 'those whom I carried and raised.' The personified Jerusalem speaks as a mother whose children were her investment; they are all gone. Chapter 2 ends with no resolution, no comfort, no reversal -- only the enormity of what YHWH has done to his people standing naked before the reader.
